turns.content: quick question — how do DJs usually structure their sets for a venue where they play the same night every week?? like keeping it fresh for the regulars but still having a vibe

Great question! For a recurring residency, the balance between consistency and freshness is key. Here's how most experienced DJs handle it:

**The Anchor Framework:**
- Keep about 30-40% of your set as "anchor" tracks — songs the regulars expect and love. These become part of your identity at that venue
- Rotate the other 60-70% with new tracks, deep cuts, and experiments
- Have a signature opener or closer that people associate with your night

**Week-to-week variety:**
- Theme nights occasionally — genre-focused, decade throwbacks, all-vinyl, etc.
- Adjust energy based on crowd size and mood — a packed Friday plays different than a slow one
- Introduce new releases gradually — test one or two new tracks per week

What venue are you playing, and what kind of crowd are you working with? That changes the approach a lot.

I'm at the Drake Hotel!! every Friday night. it's been my residency for a bit now and honestly it's the best ting that's happened to my career. the crowd there is WICKED fam like they actually know music

The Drake Hotel is a fantastic room to hold a residency. That venue attracts a really music-literate crowd — artists, creatives, industry people. They're the type who notice when you drop something unexpected and appreciate the curation.

That's actually great for you because it means you can take more risks than you would at a typical club. You don't need to play it safe with top 40 — the Drake crowd wants to discover things.

Some Drake-specific tips:

- **Read the room early**: The vibe can shift depending on what event or exhibition is happening in the building that night. The gallery crowd vs. the late-night crowd are different animals
- **Use the space**: The Drake's sound system and layout reward dynamic sets — build, pull back, build again
- **Network**: With that caliber of crowd, your Friday sets are basically a weekly showcase. Promoters, A&Rs, and other artists pass through

How long have your sets been running? And are you doing a specific time slot?

I usually do like 11pm to 2am so I get the peak hours which is MAJOR. and you're right about the networking ting — I've had so many conversations with industry people who just came through for a drink and ended up staying for my whole set!!

11pm to 2am is the prime slot — that tells me the venue trusts you with their biggest hours. That's not something they hand out casually, especially at the Drake.

Three hours is also the sweet spot for storytelling through your set. You have enough time to take the crowd on a journey:

- **11:00-11:45** — Set the tone. Mid-energy, draw people in. The early crowd is still settling
- **11:45-12:30** — Start building. This is where your deeper cuts and more experimental tracks work well. The crowd is locked in now
- **12:30-1:30** — Peak energy. Your biggest tracks, hardest drops, the moments people will remember and talk about
- **1:30-2:00** — Bring it home. A few crowd favorites, maybe something unexpected to end on. Leave them wanting more

For keeping regulars engaged week to week — do you dig for new music regularly, or do you tend to work from a set library?

oh I'm ALWAYS digging!! Bandcamp, SoundCloud, producers sending me tings. I probably add 15-20 new tracks to my library every week still. plus I play my own productions too which keeps it unique cause nobody else has those

15-20 new tracks a week is serious crate-digging. That's more than enough to keep a weekly residency fresh without ever running out of material. And dropping your own productions is a huge advantage — those are literally exclusive to your sets.

Here's a strategy to make the most of your own tracks in the residency context:

- **Test new productions at the Drake first** before releasing them anywhere. Watch the crowd reaction live — that's the most honest A&B test you'll ever get
- **If a track consistently hits**, that becomes a Drake set staple. The regulars start anticipating it
- **When you eventually release it**, those regulars become your first wave of listeners because they already have an emotional connection to the track

You're basically using the residency as a live development lab for your music. That's exactly what residencies are for.

Is the Drake gig also working out financially, or is it more of a profile-building thing for you?

it's BOTH honestly!! they pay me $650 a night which is solid bread for a Friday. not rich money but steady you know. every week I know that's coming in. and then the exposure on top of that is priceless cause people see me there and book me for other tings

$650 per night for a weekly residency is solid. That's roughly $2,600 a month guaranteed just from Fridays, which is a real financial foundation. A lot of artists underestimate how important that stability is — it lets you take creative risks with the rest of your week because you're not scrambling for income.

And you're right about the exposure multiplier. A residency at a respected venue like the Drake works like compound interest:

- Week 1: People discover you
- Week 4: They're bringing friends
- Week 8: Promoters are reaching out because they keep hearing your name
- Week 12: You're associated with the venue's brand, which carries its own weight

The combination of steady income PLUS a reputation-building platform is honestly the ideal setup for a working DJ/producer. You've got your base covered financially and you're growing your profile at the same time.

Are you booking other gigs on top of the Friday residency, or keeping the rest of the week mostly for production?

I try to keep Saturdays open for one-off bookings and then the rest of the week is lab time!! production, mixing, writing. I need those days in the lab or I go crazy. the Drake gives me the structure and the weekdays give me the creative freedom

That's a really disciplined schedule and it's set up well. Friday residency for income and exposure, Saturday open for bonus gigs, weekdays for creating. You've essentially built yourself a sustainable career structure.

A couple things to think about to maximize that setup:

- **Saturday gigs**: Since these are your variable income, try to push for higher rates on one-offs. You can justify premium pricing because you're in demand — you have a residency at the Drake, not just freelancing around
- **Weekday production**: Block your time. Maybe Monday-Wednesday is studio work, Thursday is admin/promo/digging for new tracks, so you're fresh and prepared for Friday
- **Document the Drake sets**: Even just recording your sets on your phone or a simple USB recorder from the mixer. Over time, you build an archive you can use for mixes, promos, or just tracking what works with the crowd

Are you recording your Friday sets at all?
I record most of them yeah!! I've been thinking about putting some up as mixes on SoundCloud or Mixcloud to reach people who can't make it to the Drake on Fridays. good idea or nah??

Absolutely yes. Posting residency mixes is one of the best promotional tools you have. Here's why:

- **Proof of skill**: A live recorded set shows you can actually DJ, not just produce. It demonstrates reading a crowd, mixing, track selection — the full package
- **Extended reach**: People in other cities or countries can hear what a Yoli Friday night sounds like. That leads to out-of-town bookings
- **Content consistency**: A weekly residency means a weekly mix to post. Regular content keeps you visible

**Mixcloud** is probably better for full sets because of licensing — they have deals with labels so you won't get copyright strikes. SoundCloud is better for shorter highlight mixes or sets featuring mostly your own productions.

Tips for posting:
- Give each mix a name and a short description of the vibe that night
- Post consistently — even if not every week, at least twice a month
- Tag the tracks if you can, people love tracklists

This turns one Friday night into content that works for you all week. Do it.
ok I'm doing it!! gonna start this Friday. record the set, edit it a bit on the weekday, post on Mixcloud by Tuesday. rinse and repeat. the riddim never stops ahlie!! thanks for the game plan fam

That's a perfect workflow — perform Friday, edit Monday, post Tuesday, repeat. Consistent and manageable without eating into your production time.

You've got a really strong setup with the Drake residency. Steady money, profile building, and now a content pipeline. Go crush it this Friday and get that first mix up. The riddim indeed never stops! Good luck!
